perturbation.original_text: The Information will be kept confidential and shall not without your prior written consent, be disclosed by us, or by our agents, representatives or employees, in any manner whatsover, in whole or in part, and shall not be used by us, our agents, representatives or employees, other than in connection with the transaction described above.
perturbation.changed_text: The Information will be kept confidential, but may be disclosed by us, or by our agents, representatives or employees if deemed necessary, in whole or in part, and may be used by us, our agents, representatives or employees, for purposes beyond the transaction described above.
perturbation.explanation: This change contradicts the confidentiality clause, making the obligations to protect the information less strict, creating uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text weakens the confidentiality clause, potentially enabling the misappropriation of trade secrets, which violates the Economic Espionage Act.
perturbation.justification: The original text strictly prohibits disclosure of information without prior written consent, while the changed text allows disclosure if 'deemed necessary' and permits use 'for purposes beyond the transaction described above.' This directly contradicts the initial confidentiality obligation by introducing broad exceptions, effectively negating the protection offered by the original clause. While the scraped law snippet 1 references the Economic Espionage Act, the core contradiction lies in the explicit allowance of disclosure and use beyond the initial agreement, undermining the confidentiality provision. The change weakens the contract's enforceability regarding confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: Without your prior written consent, we and our Representatives will not communicate with any person or entity that is a party to any agreement with the Business or any possible transaction between us and the Business involving the Business.
perturbation.changed_text: We and our Representatives may communicate with any person or entity that is a party to any agreement with the Business or any possible transaction between us and the Business involving the Business, without your consent.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the restriction on communication with parties related to the business, which could involve improper interference with contractual relations.
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing communication with parties without consent, the modified text increases the risk of tortious interference with existing contracts, as Pennsylvania law recognizes this tort when a party intentionally interferes with a contractual relationship.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes a restriction on communication, potentially leading to interference with contractual relations. The LLM explanation states this could be 'tortious interference with existing contracts, as Pennsylvania law recognizes this tort when a party intentionally interferes with a contractual relationship.' While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing and Snippet 2 is a sign-in page, the LLM explanation combined with the perturbation explanation provides sufficient reasoning to indicate a contradiction, as the change directly opposes the principle of avoiding intentional interference with contractual relationships.

perturbation.original_text: Without your prior written consent, we will not for a period of one year from the date hereof directly or indirectly solicit for employment any person who is now employed by you or any of your subsidiaries who is identified by us as a result of our invastigation of the Business.
perturbation.changed_text: We may, for a period of one year from the date hereof, directly or indirectly solicit for employment any person who is now employed by you or any of your subsidiaries, regardless of how they are identified.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes restrictions on soliciting employees, which could be considered improper solicitation under Pennsylvania law if it involves confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The amended clause permits solicitation of employees, even if it leads to the disclosure or use of trade secrets, potentially violating Pennsylvania's Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5302 (2022) - Definitions :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5302 - Definitions Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5302 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5302. Definitions. The following words and phrases when used in this chapter shall have the meanings given to them in this section unless the context clearly indicates otherwise: "Improper means." Includes, but is not limited to, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation." Includes: (1) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. "Person." A natural person, corporation,
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the restriction on soliciting employees and states that the solicitor may solicit employees 'regardless of how they are identified'. The law snippet defines 'improper means' to include 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By soliciting employees regardless of how they are identified, the changed text allows for soliciting employees even if that inducement breaches a duty to maintain secrecy or involves the misappropriation of trade secrets, thus directly contradicting the Pennsylvania statute by potentially facilitating actions defined as 'improper means' of acquiring trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that we or anyone to whom we transmit the Information pursuant to this Agreement becomes legally compelled to disclose any of the Information, we will provide you with prompt written notice and oral notice so that you may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that we or anyone to whom we transmit the Information becomes legally compelled to disclose any of the Information, we are not required to notify you, and we will disclose the information immediately.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the obligation to provide notice before disclosing information under legal compulsion, conflicting with standard legal practices to seek protective orders.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the notification requirement and obligation to seek a protective order, the modified text impedes the other party's ability to protect confidential information during discovery, conflicting with the spirit of Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 26(c), which allows for protective orders to shield against disclosure of confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement to notify the other party when legally compelled to disclose information, and also removes the obligation to seek a protective order, directly contradicting the principles of protecting confidential information during discovery, as supported by the Law Explanation referencing Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 26(c). Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions Rule 26, which governs discovery and implicitly acknowledges the possibility of information being 'protected from disclosure,' suggesting mechanisms like protective orders are contemplated within the legal framework. The original contract provided a mechanism for seeking such protection, which is removed entirely in the changed text. This removal creates a significant legal contradiction.
